([11 & 12 Geo. 6](/wiki/11_%26_12_Geo._6 "11 & 12 Geo. 6"). c. 65) was an [act](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Act of Parliament (United Kingdom)") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") that altered the law relating to parliamentary and local elections. It is noteworthy for abolishing [multi-member districts](/wiki/Multi-member_constituencies_in_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Multi-member constituencies in the Parliament of the United Kingdom"), abolishing the [university constituencies](/wiki/University_constituency "University constituency"), abolishing [plural voting](/wiki/Plural_voting "Plural voting") and [single transferable voting](/wiki/Single_transferable_voting "Single transferable voting") for parliamentary elections; and for again increasing the number of members overall, in this case to 613.

The **Representation of the People Act 1948** ([11 & 12 Geo. 6](/wiki/11_%26_12_Geo._6 "11 & 12 Geo. 6"). c. 65) was an [act](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Act of Parliament (United Kingdom)") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") that altered the law relating to parliamentary and local elections. It is noteworthy for abolishing [multi-member districts](/wiki/Multi-member_constituencies_in_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Multi-member constituencies in the Parliament of the United Kingdom"), abolishing the [university constituencies](/wiki/University_constituency "University constituency"), abolishing [plural voting](/wiki/Plural_voting "Plural voting") and [single transferable voting](/wiki/Single_transferable_voting "Single transferable voting") for parliamentary elections; and for again increasing the number of members overall, in this case to 613.

## Provisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Provisions")]

### Part I: Parliamentary franchise and its exercise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Part I: Parliamentary franchise and its exercise")]

Part I of the act declared that in future the United Kingdom would be divided into single-member [borough constituencies](/wiki/Borough_constituencies "Borough constituencies") and [county constituencies](/wiki/County_constituencies "County constituencies"). These terms replaced the former designations of [parliamentary borough](/wiki/Parliamentary_borough "Parliamentary borough")/division of a parliamentary borough and parliamentary county/division of a parliamentary county (in Scotland "burgh constituencies" replaced [parliamentary burghs](/wiki/Parliamentary_burghs "Parliamentary burghs")). There were to be 613 such constituencies, in place of the 591 under previous legislation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

These were to be the only constituencies, and the act thus abolished the [university constituencies](/wiki/University_constituencies "University constituencies"); thus graduates of [universities](/wiki/Universities "Universities") (about 7% of the electorate)[[1]](#cite_note-4) no longer had the right to vote in two constituencies. Constituencies which had been represented by more than one MP were also abolished.[[2]](#cite_note-fact-5)[[3]](#cite_note-6)

Persons eligible to vote were to be British subjects of "full age" (21 years) and "not subject to any legal incapacity to vote", provided that they were registered to vote in the constituency. Each voter was only permitted to cast their vote in one constituency, even if for some reason they were registered in more than one. The arrangements which had given plural votes to electors who met a property qualification because of their business or shop premises were abolished.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Each constituency was to have an electoral registration officer, who was to compile the [electoral register](/wiki/Electoral_register "Electoral register"). In [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), this officer was the clerk of the appropriate [county](/wiki/County_council "County council") or [borough council](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough"); in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), it was the assessor[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] of a [county](/wiki/Counties_of_Scotland "Counties of Scotland") or [large burgh](/wiki/Large_burgh "Large burgh"); and in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") it was the town clerk of the county borough of [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") or the secretary of the county council. An electoral register was to be published in Spring and Autumn of each year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Qualifications for an elector to be registered were set out, with residence in the constituency on a specified date being the principal requirement. There was an additional "service qualification" for members of the armed forces and other persons outside the state on diplomatic or other Crown business, and their spouses. Electors were to vote in person, except in exceptional circumstances in which a proxy vote might be permitted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Each constituency was to be divided into polling districts by the registration officer, who was also to designate polling places within each district. Rules were laid down for the process: for instance each [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in an English or Welsh county constituency was to be a separate district. Where a group of thirty electors felt that they were not provided with a convenient polling place, they were entitled to [petition](/wiki/Right_to_petition "Right to petition") the Secretary of State for a review.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The procedures for recounts and for choosing the winning candidate by lot in the event of a tie were laid down. A candidate who received less than an eighth of the total number of votes cast would forfeit their monetary deposit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Finally Part I dealt with the appointment and duties of the [returning officer](/wiki/Returning_officer "Returning officer"). In England and Wales these were to be either the [high sheriff](/wiki/High_sheriff "High sheriff") of a county, the sheriff of a [county corporate](/wiki/County_corporate "County corporate"), the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") of a borough or the chairman of an [urban district](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") council, as appointed by the [Home Secretary](/wiki/Home_Secretary "Home Secretary"). In Scotland the returning officer was to be a sheriff of a local [sheriffdom](/wiki/Sheriffdom "Sheriffdom"), and in Northern Ireland the under-sheriff of a county or county borough.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Part II: General provisions as to local government franchise and its exercise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Part II: General provisions as to local government franchise and its exercise")]

The electorate for local elections was larger than that for parliamentary elections. Apart from those resident in the district, there was an additional "non-resident qualification" to vote where an owner or tenant occupied rateable land or premises therein of the yearly value of not less than ten pounds. The electoral registration officer appointed under Part I of the act was also to compile a local government register, although the two registers could be combined, with the names of those persons registered only as local government electors marked. Electors were not permitted to be registered more than once in a single local government district, even if they occupied multiple premises. There was no prohibition on voting in different local authority areas, however.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Polling districts were to be delineated and polling places designated by the registration officer. In the absence of other arrangements these were to be identical to those used for parliamentary elections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Part III: Corrupt and illegal practices and other provisions as to election campaigns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Part III: Corrupt and illegal practices and other provisions as to election campaigns")]

Part III of the act set new limits for the expenses that candidates were permitted to pay their election agent. In a county constituency this was to be [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")450, plus 2[d](/wiki/Penny_(British_pre-decimal_coin) "Penny (British pre-decimal coin)") for each name in the electoral register; in borough constituencies it was to be £450 plus 1½d for each elector.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Among other restrictions, no supporter of a candidate was permitted to use a motor vehicle to bring an elector to the polls, or to loan or rent such a vehicle to an elector, unless the vehicle was first registered with the returning officer. There was to be a limit of one vehicle per 1,500 electors in a county constituency and 2,500 in a borough constituency. The broadcast of any programme relating to an election on a radio station other than one operated by the [British Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/British_Broadcasting_Corporation "British Broadcasting Corporation") was also prohibited. This prohibition extended to broadcasters outside the state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Each candidate was allowed to send an [election address](/wiki/Election_address "Election address") to each elector post free, and was entitled to the use of a room in a publicly funded school in which to hold meetings.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Part IV: Special provisions as to local elections in England and Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Part IV: Special provisions as to local elections in England and Wales")]

Part IV altered the dates for the holding of local elections in England and Wales. County councillors were to be elected in the first week of April, and all other councillors in the first week of May. All borough elections were to be held on the same day, [set by the Home Secretary](/wiki/Powers_of_the_home_secretary "Powers of the home secretary"). The date for other elections was to be set by the appropriate county council. The change of dates meant that the borough elections due in November 1948 were postponed until the following May. [Mayors](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor")' and council chairmen's terms of office were extended until the first meeting held after the rescheduled elections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The constitution of the [London County Council](/wiki/London_County_Council "London County Council") was slightly altered: previously two councillors were elected for electoral divisions corresponding to each parliamentary constituency in the [County of London](/wiki/County_of_London "County of London"), with an additional four for the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London"). With the reorganisation of constituencies in the county by the act, the City lost its special position, being combined with [Westminster](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Westminster "Metropolitan Borough of Westminster") in a [single electoral division](/wiki/Cities_of_London_and_Westminster_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cities of London and Westminster (UK Parliament constituency)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Part V: Special provisions as to local government elections in Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Part V: Special provisions as to local government elections in Scotland")]

The dates for holding local elections in Scotland was also altered:

* Elections for county councillors for landward[[d]](#cite_note-8) areas of counties and for district councillors were to be held on the second Tuesday of May in 1949 and every three years thereafter.
* Burgh councillors were to be elected on the first Tuesday in May 1949 and annually thereafter.
* County councillors representing burghs were to be elected in May 1949 following the council election, and every three years thereafter.

County, district and burgh elections due in 1948 were postponed until 1949, and councillors due to retire were to continue in office. This also applied to county conveners, burgh provosts, honorary treasurers of burghs and chairmen of district councils.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Part VI: General

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Part VI: General")]

The final part of the act listed the duties of the registration officer and established an appeals procedure for persons excluded from the register. It also allowed for funds to be made available for the registration process.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Short title, commencement and extent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Short title, commencement and extent")]

Section 80(6) of the act provided that part V of the act, and part VI as relates to part V, would come into force on 1 October 1948.

Section 81 of the act provided that the act may be cited as the "Representation of the People Act, 1948" and may be cited as a [Representation of the People Act](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act "Representation of the People Act").

## Number of constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Number of constituencies")]

Schedule I set out the names, number and constitution of the constituencies, which replaced those created by the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") ([7 & 8 Geo. 5](/wiki/7_%26_8_Geo._5 "7 & 8 Geo. 5"). c. 64) and [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920") ([10 & 11 Geo. 5](/wiki/10_%26_11_Geo._5 "10 & 11 Geo. 5"). c. 67). In a few counties where there had been an exceptional increase in the electorate since 1918, additional constituencies had been created for the [1945 general election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") by the [House of Commons (Redistribution of Seats) Act 1944](/wiki/House_of_Commons_(Redistribution_of_Seats)_Act_1944 "House of Commons (Redistribution of Seats) Act 1944") ([7 & 8 Geo. 6](/wiki/7_%26_8_Geo._6 "7 & 8 Geo. 6"). c. 41) as a temporary measure.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The changes to constituencies were to be introduced from [the next general election](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election"), which was held on 23 February 1950. [By-elections held before then](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_by-elections_(1931%E2%80%931950)#1945–1950_Parliament "List of United Kingdom by-elections (1931–1950)") used the old constituencies.

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Sections 19 and 43 of the act were repealed by section 206 of, and part I of schedule 9 to, the [Representation of the People Act 1983](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1983 "Representation of the People Act 1983").

7. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") returned 7 MPs as [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Norwich (UK Parliament constituency)") returned two members.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** [East Sussex](/wiki/East_Sussex "East Sussex") returned 7 MPs as [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Brighton (UK Parliament constituency)") returned two members.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** The Kincardineshire burgh of [Inverbervie](/wiki/Inverbervie "Inverbervie") was included in the [Montrose District of Burghs](/wiki/Montrose_Burghs_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Montrose Burghs (UK Parliament constituency)") in Forfarshire.
